commands.author: clawsweeper[bot]
commands.repair_reason: - [P1] The remaining work is maintainer design/upgrade-risk selection and contributor current-head real behavior proof, not a narrow automated repair.; Cleared: No concrete security or supply-chain concern was found; the diff changes local memory indexing/schema/tests and adds no dependencies, workflows, downloads, permissions, or secret handling. (sha=0fe93c1712ad20e808c134fb09871c2d0500e4a7)
commands.review_summary:
The branch adds a derived path FTS table for memory indexes, updates indexing/search/schema repair/reindex publication paths to populate and merge path hits, and adds regression coverage.

PR surface: Source +344, Tests +665. Total +1009 across 10 files.

Reproducibility: yes. Source inspection shows current main indexes only chunk text in the body FTS table while storing path as unindexed metadata, matching the filename/date-token search failure.
commands.review_followup: - [P1] The remaining work is maintainer design/upgrade-risk selection and contributor current-head real behavior proof, not a narrow automated repair.

target.title: fix(memory-core): include path in FTS indexed text so filename tokens are searchable
target.body:
## What problem does this PR solve?

`openclaw memory search` can rank filename/date-token queries poorly or miss them when the filename token only lives in the derived path FTS index. The current PR was also stale against the canonical `memory_index_*` table migration, so the original fix needed to be rebased onto the current schema owner instead of keeping the old `chunks_*` wiring.

## Why does this matter now?

The linked issue is a user-visible memory lookup failure: a clean memory index and working content search still did not reliably return the expected memory file for a filename/date-token query such as `2026-06-17-1649`. Without this repair, users can have the right memory file indexed but still fail to retrieve it by the filename/date token they know.

## What is the intended outcome?

Filename/date-token keyword searches should find the matching memory file while body-text BM25 ranking remains based on body text only. `memory_index_chunks` remains canonical, and both derived FTS tables are repaired or published from that canonical table.

## What is intentionally out of scope?

This does not change memory file format, vector search scoring, providers, auth, network behavior, gateway startup behavior, or the public memory API contract.

## Summary

- Fix classification: root-cause compatibility repair for memory FTS path search on the current canonical memory index schema.
- Maintainer-ready confidence: high for the local code path because the update adds/keeps regressions for search merging, schema drift repair, legacy source migration, and full shadow reindex path FTS publication, and it passes the CI-equivalent lint checks that were failing on the stale PR head.
- Root cause: filename/date-token matches live in a derived path FTS index, but search/schema/publish paths were not consistently treating `memory_index_chunks` as the source of truth for both `memory_index_chunks_fts` and `memory_index_chunks_fts_path`. That could leave path hits missing after schema repair/full reindex, or let body hits consume the requested limit before filename hits were merged.
- What changed: memory indexing now writes both the body FTS table and the separate path FTS table; stale cleanup removes both derived FTS rows; schema repair rebuilds drifted FTS rows from `memory_index_chunks`; full shadow reindex publishing copies `memory_index_chunks_fts_path`; keyword search merges body/path rows, keeps the best rank per chunk id, sorts the merged rows, and only then applies the final limit.
- Why it matters / User impact: `openclaw memory search "2026-06-17-1649"` can find `memory/2026-06-17-1649.md` by filename/date token without prepending path text into the body index or weakening body BM25 relevance.
- Architecture / source-of-truth check: `memory_index_chunks` remains canonical; FTS tables are derived indexes; schema setup and full reindex publish own derived-index repair; search owns merging body/path keyword candidates before result limiting.
- Patch quality notes: the larger diff is from carrying the existing PR through the current canonical table migration, not from adding an unrelated feature. The `readCount` fallback only normalizes an empty SQLite `COUNT(*)` wrapper row in schema repair; it does not hide missing canonical chunk data or return fake search results.
- Related open PR scan: this updates my existing open PR #94114 for linked issue #94102. Sibling PR #94135 remains a maintainer selection signal; this PR now uses the current canonical `memory_index_*` schema rather than the stale pre-rebase table shape.

## Linked context

Closes #94102

Related #94114

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: memory search should find indexed memory files by filename/date tokens through the memory index/search path while preserving body BM25 ranking.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw memory indexing/search behavior and SQLite FTS schema repair were validated on the committed head. This update did not exercise an external OAuth/provider-backed live account because the changed code path is local memory indexing/search/schema repair, not provider auth or network I/O.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

  ```bash
  pnpm exec vitest run extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-db.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-search.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/index.test.ts packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/memory-schema.test.ts
  pnpm lint
  pnpm run lint:extensions:bundled
  ```

- Evidence after fix:

  ```text
  Test Files  4 passed (4)
  Tests  97 passed (97)
  Duration  49.92s

  $ node scripts/run-oxlint-shards.mjs
  [oxlint:core] finished
  [oxlint:extensions] finished
  [oxlint:scripts] finished

  $ node scripts/run-bundled-extension-oxlint.mjs
  [qa-channel boundary dts] fresh; skipping
  [discord boundary dts] fresh; skipping
  [slack boundary dts] fresh; skipping
  [whatsapp boundary dts] fresh; skipping
  ```

- Observed result after fix: the current head passes the canonical memory search/schema regressions, including date-token filename lookup, merge-before-limit behavior, drift repair from `memory_index_chunks`, legacy source migration, and shadow path FTS publication.
- What was not tested: no external OAuth/provider-backed account, gateway session, provider auth flow, or network behavior was exercised for this update.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: the available proof is local memory manager/schema validation plus lint. The prior runtime CLI proof for this PR demonstrated `openclaw memory index --force` followed by `openclaw memory search "2026-06-17-1649" --json --max-results 3` returning `memory/2026-06-17-1649.md`; after this rebase, the new proof focuses on the canonical schema path and CI-visible failures.

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?

```bash
pnpm exec vitest run extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-db.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-search.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/index.test.ts packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/memory-schema.test.ts
pnpm lint
pnpm run lint:extensions:bundled
```

Target test files:

- `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-db.test.ts`
- `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/manager-search.test.ts`
- `extensions/memory-core/src/memory/index.test.ts`
- `packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/memory-schema.test.ts`

What regression coverage was added or updated?

- Body/path FTS candidates are merged before the requested result limit is applied, so a filename hit is not dropped when body matches fill the limit.
- `memory_index_chunks_fts` / `memory_index_chunks_fts_path` row-count or content drift is repaired from canonical `memory_index_chunks`.
- Full shadow memory reindex publication copies `memory_index_chunks_fts_path`, so filename/date-token rows survive in-place reindex publish.
- Legacy memory stores without `source` columns migrate into canonical rows with the default `memory` source before rebuilding derived FTS rows.
- Merged keyword rows use `toSorted`, avoiding in-place ordering mutation while preserving rank order.

- Focused regression command after this patch: the four memory manager/schema test files above were run together against the committed head.
- Scenario locked in: canonical chunk rows remain the source of truth while body and path FTS tables are repaired/published separately and merged before result limiting.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: these tests exercise the changed search merge/rank behavior, the schema-owner repair path, the legacy migration path, and the full reindex publish path directly without requiring provider embeddings or gateway-only startup proof.

## CI attribution

- Before this update, PR #94114 was stale/conflicting with current main and had visible failing checks for `check-lint` and `check-additional-extension-bundled` on the old head.
- This update rebases the fix onto the current canonical `memory_index_*` table migration and locally passes the matching lint checks: `pnpm lint` and `pnpm run lint:extensions:bundled`.
- The old CI checkout is no longer aligned with this new head; fresh remote checks should be evaluated after this branch update lands.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? `Yes`

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? `Yes`

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? `No`

What is the highest-risk area?

Existing local memory SQLite stores may have derived FTS tables that are missing rows, use older FTS layouts, or were built before `source` existed on legacy memory tables.

How is that risk mitigated?

The repair treats `memory_index_chunks` as source of truth and rebuilds only derived FTS rows whose row counts or row contents no longer match canonical chunks. It does not rewrite memory files, vectors, auth/config, provider state, or canonical chunk rows. Body-text FTS remains separate from path FTS, so filename lookup no longer changes body BM25 document-length normalization.

## Current review state

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?

- RF-001 / waiting-on-author state: addressed on the author side by rebasing the PR, fixing the stale canonical table conflict, and passing the local checks that correspond to the visible failures. The label itself remains for the maintainer/bot to refresh.
- RF-002 / compatibility design: still intentionally visible as a maintainer design judgment, because the PR maintains a separate derived path FTS table and schema repair path. The implementation is now aligned with canonical `memory_index_*` tables.
- RF-003 / stale branch and canonical table drift: addressed by moving indexing, cleanup, search, schema repair, and full reindex publish to the canonical memory index tables.
- RF-004 / sibling PR #94135: disclosed as a maintainer selection signal; this PR presents the canonical-table version of the fix.
- RF-005 / rebase and lint author work: addressed locally with the updated commit plus `pnpm lint` and `pnpm run lint:extensions:bundled`.
- RF-006 / path FTS canonical queries: addressed by guarding path/body FTS rows against `memory_index_chunks` and using canonical fixture/test coverage.
- RF-007 / keyword row ordering: addressed by using `toSorted` for merged keyword rows.
- ClawSweeper commit review finding / same-count FTS drift: addressed by rebuilding derived body/path FTS rows when row contents no longer match canonical `memory_index_chunks`, even if row counts already match.

What is the next action?

Maintainers can compare this canonical-table approach with sibling PR #94135 and let the normal remote checks refresh on the updated branch.
target.author: zhangguiping-xydt

commands.reason: PR is not opted into ClawSweeper autofix or automerge
